---
title: "Generalised additive mixed models: closing the loop"
---

A **GAMM** has smooth terms *and* random effects in one model. But the
[GAM chapter](gam.qmd#sec-hinge) already showed that a smooth **is** a random
effect — both are a quadratic penalty block $\gamma_k^{\top}S\gamma_k$ on a slice
of the coefficients. A GAM already fits a *sum* of penalty blocks. So a GAMM is "a
GAM with one more kind of block", needs **no new solver**, and the single
[Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd) loop estimates every smoothing parameter *and*
every variance component together. The one new idea here: a single penalised
smooth, looked at the right way, already *contains* a fixed effect and a random
effect — so the GAM and the LME were never two models. This is where the ladder
folds shut.

Rung 5 of [the ladder](index.qmd). This chapter does not add machinery; it
removes a distinction. Everything you need has already been built — by the end you
will see all of it as a single equation with interchangeable parts.

## A question that needs both halves {#sec-motivation}

Here is a study that will not fit on any single earlier rung. You have cortical
thickness measured in a brain region for a few hundred people, scanned at four
different sites, several of them more than once. You want to answer two questions
at once:

1. **How does thickness change with age?** Not linearly — thinning accelerates in
   later life, so the population trajectory is a *curve* you do not want to
   pre-specify. That is a job for a **smooth** $f(\text{age})$ — the [GAM](gam.qmd).
2. **How much does the scanner matter?** Two sites with different hardware give
   systematically offset measurements, and you do not care about *those four
   particular machines* — you care about the population of sites they represent,
   and about not letting a site with many scans dominate the curve. That is a job
   for a **random intercept** per site — the [LME](lme.qmd).

You cannot answer (1) honestly without (2): if site offsets leak into the age
curve, you will read scanner drift as biology. And you cannot answer (2) without
(1): a wrong functional form for age dumps its residual into the site effects. The
two terms have to be estimated *together*, in one model:

$$
g\big(\mathbb{E}[y]\big) = \underbrace{X\beta}_{\text{parametric}}
  + \underbrace{\textstyle\sum_k f_k(x_k)}_{\text{smooths}}
  + \underbrace{Z b}_{\text{random effects}},
\qquad f_k = B_k\gamma_k,\quad b\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_b^2 I).
$$ {#eq-gamm}

Here $X\beta$ is anything entering linearly (the grand mean, sex, a linear motion
covariate), each $f_k=B_k\gamma_k$ is a basis-expanded smooth with its own
roughness penalty, and $Zb$ is the per-site random intercept with $Z$ the one-hot
site indicator. A model with both smooths and random effects under a link $g$ is a
**generalised additive mixed model**. We will keep returning to this age-by-site
example.

The promise of this chapter is that @eq-gamm looks like three different kinds of
thing glued together, but is really *one* kind of thing repeated. Let us earn that.

## A smooth already is a fixed effect plus a random effect {#sec-unification}

Recall the one fact the GAM chapter left you with: a roughness penalty
$\lambda\,\gamma^{\top}S\gamma$ on a smooth's coefficients is *mathematically the
same object* as the shrinkage a random effect feels (see
[the GAM penalty](gam.qmd#sec-penalty) and the [grand equivalence](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence)).
We now make that concrete enough to *see*, because it is the hinge the whole
chapter swings on.

**Start from the penalty matrix and ask what it cannot see.** A second-difference
roughness penalty measures *wiggliness*: $\gamma^{\top}S\gamma$ is large when the
smooth curves sharply and zero when it does not curve at all. But a straight line
does not curve. So a constant and a linear trend both give $\gamma^{\top}S\gamma=0$
— the penalty is *blind* to them. In linear-algebra terms, the constant-and-linear
functions are exactly the **null space** of $S$ (the vectors $\gamma$ with
$S\gamma=0$). For a $d$-th order difference penalty the null space is all
polynomials of degree $<d$; for the common second-difference penalty it is
$\{\text{constant},\ \text{linear}\}$, dimension two.

This splits the smooth's coefficient vector into two parts that the penalty treats
completely differently:

$$
\gamma = \underbrace{\gamma_{\text{null}}}_{\text{unpenalised}}
       + \underbrace{\gamma_{\text{range}}}_{\text{penalised}},
\qquad
\gamma^{\top}S\gamma = \gamma_{\text{range}}^{\top}S\gamma_{\text{range}}.
$$ {#eq-decomp}

The null-space part $\gamma_{\text{null}}$ pays no penalty no matter how large it
gets: it is free to take whatever value least-squares wants. **That is exactly how
a fixed effect behaves** — estimated with no shrinkage. The range-space part
$\gamma_{\text{range}}$ pays $\lambda\,\gamma_{\text{range}}^{\top}S\gamma_{\text{range}}$,
which is the same as putting a mean-zero Gaussian prior on it with precision
$\lambda$ — i.e. a variance $\sigma^2 = 1/\lambda$. **That is exactly how a random
effect behaves** — shrunk toward zero, by an amount the data choose.

So read @eq-decomp again, slowly: a single penalised smooth is the *sum* of an
unpenalised low-order trend (a **fixed effect**) and a penalised mean-zero wiggle
(a **random effect** with variance $1/\lambda$). The GAM did not contain a random
effect by analogy; it contained one literally. @fig-gamm-decomp shows the split on
a concrete curve — the same wiggly smooth pulled apart into the straight line the
penalty ignores and the mean-zero wiggle it shrinks.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gamm-decomp
#| fig-cap: "A penalised smooth splits into its null-space part — an unpenalised straight-line *fixed* effect — plus its range-space part — a mean-zero *random* wiggle with variance $1/\\lambda$. The two sum back to the whole curve. The GAM's smooth and the LME's random effect are the same object relabelled."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
x = np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 200)

# A wiggly smooth = a linear trend plus a few sinusoidal bumps.
trend = 0.8 + 1.6 * x                      # the low-order (null-space) part
wiggle = (0.35 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * 1.5 * x)
          + 0.20 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * 3.0 * x + 0.7)
          - 0.15 * np.cos(2 * np.pi * 2.0 * x))
# Project the linear part OUT of the wiggle so the split is the genuine
# null-space / range-space decomposition: the wiggle is orthogonal to {1, x}.
P = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(x), x])   # null space of S (deg <= 1)
beta_hat, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(P, wiggle, rcond=None)
wiggle = wiggle - P @ beta_hat
whole = trend + wiggle

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11, 3.4), sharey=True)
ax[0].plot(x, whole, color="C3", lw=2)
ax[0].set_title("the smooth  $f(x)$")
ax[1].plot(x, trend, color="C0", lw=2)
ax[1].set_title("fixed part: null space of $S$\n(the straight-line trend)")
ax[2].plot(x, wiggle, color="C2", lw=2)
ax[2].axhline(0.0, color="0.6", lw=0.8, ls="--")
ax[2].set_title("random part: penalised wiggle\n(mean-zero, variance $1/\\lambda$)")
ax[0].set_ylabel("response")
for a in ax:
    a.set_xlabel("age (scaled)")
    a.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
fig.suptitle("A penalised smooth = fixed straight line  +  random mean-zero wiggle",
             y=1.04, fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

This is the **mixed-model representation of a penalised spline** (Ruppert, Wand &
Carroll). It is not a trick for visualisation; it is *the* reason GAM-fitting and
LME-fitting share code. Fit the smooth, and you have fitted a tiny mixed model:
its fixed effect is the trend, its one random effect is the wiggle, and the
smoothing parameter $\lambda$ *is* the random-effect precision $1/\sigma^2$. Run
that statement backward and a random intercept is itself a smooth whose penalty
happens to be the identity. There is no boundary between the two rungs to cross.

## Everything is a penalty block

With the hinge in hand, the rest of the model assembles itself. Write the two
regularised terms of our age-by-site model side by side:

| term | penalty | strength |
|---|---|---|
| smooth $f_k$ | $\lambda_k\,\gamma_k^{\top}S\gamma_k$ (roughness) | $\lambda_k$ |
| random effect $b$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sigma_b^2}\,b^{\top}b$ (ridge) | $1/\sigma_b^2$ |

They are the *same kind of object* — a quadratic penalty on a block of
coefficients, with a strength to be chosen — differing only in the matrix inside:
a difference/curvature operator for a smooth, the identity for a random intercept.
Because each term owns a **disjoint** slice of the coefficient vector, stacking
them amounts to placing each block on the diagonal of one big penalty matrix:

$$
S(\lambda) = \operatorname{diag}\big(\lambda_1 S_1,\ \lambda_2 S_2,\ \dots,\ \lambda_K S_K\big),
$$ {#eq-blockdiag}

with the unpenalised parametric head contributing a zero block (it pays no
penalty, so it sits in the null space of $S(\lambda)$ — a fixed effect, exactly as
[above](#sec-unification)). @fig-gamm-blocks shows this matrix for our example: two smooths and a
random intercept, each a labelled block, the parametric head a dark zero block.
The "everything is a penalty block" slogan is now something you can literally look
at.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gamm-blocks
#| fig-cap: "The combined block-diagonal penalty $S(\\lambda)=\\mathrm{diag}(\\lambda_1 S_1,\\dots)$ for a GAMM with two P-spline smooths (age, motion; second-difference roughness blocks) and a per-site random intercept (an identity ridge), plus an unpenalised parametric head (the dark zero block). Smooths and random effects are *the same kind of block*; only the matrix inside differs."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def diff_penalty(k, order=2):
    """k x k second-difference roughness penalty  D^T D."""
    D = np.diff(np.eye(k), n=order, axis=0)
    return D.T @ D

lam_age, lam_motion, lam_site = 8.0, 2.0, 25.0   # smoothing params / RE precision
k_age, k_motion, q_site, p_par = 8, 8, 6, 2

blocks = [np.zeros((p_par, p_par)),              # unpenalised parametric head
          lam_age * diff_penalty(k_age),
          lam_motion * diff_penalty(k_motion),
          lam_site * np.eye(q_site)]             # the random-effect ridge
sizes = [p_par, k_age, k_motion, q_site]
p = sum(sizes)
S = np.zeros((p, p))
off = 0
for B, s in zip(blocks, sizes):
    S[off:off + s, off:off + s] = B
    off += s

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.2, 4.8))
im = ax.imshow(S, cmap="viridis")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04, label="penalty weight")
labels = ["parametric\n(unpenalised)", "smooth: age", "smooth: motion", "RE: site"]
off = 0
for lab, s in zip(labels, sizes):
    centre = off + s / 2 - 0.5
    ax.text(centre, centre, lab, ha="center", va="center",
            color="white", fontsize=8, fontweight="bold")
    off += s
    if off < p:
        ax.axhline(off - 0.5, color="white", lw=0.8)
        ax.axvline(off - 0.5, color="white", lw=0.8)
ax.set_title("$S(\\lambda)=\\mathrm{diag}(\\lambda_1 S_1,\\dots)$:\n"
             "one block per term, smooths and random effects alike")
ax.set_xlabel("coefficient index")
ax.set_ylabel("coefficient index")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Stack the corresponding designs left-to-right into one combined $X=[\,1\mid X_{\text{par}}\mid B_1\mid B_2\mid Z\,]$,
and the entire GAMM collapses into the single penalised normal-equations solve we
have used since the GLM — the [penalised IRLS](glm.qmd#sec-irls) step:

$$
\Big(X^{\top}WX + S(\lambda)\Big)\,\gamma = X^{\top}Wz ,
$$ {#eq-gamm-solve}

with $W$ the IRLS working weights and $z$ the working response (so a non-Gaussian
link — Poisson counts, binomial proportions — costs nothing extra; it is the GLM's
inner loop unchanged). The same Cholesky factorisation that solves a GLM solves a
GAMM; the only difference is what got added to $X^{\top}WX$.

### How `nitrix` assembles it

This is exactly the shape the library builds. `gam_fit` (in `gam.py`) takes a
*list* of `smooths`, and its `_assemble` helper does precisely @eq-blockdiag: it
concatenates each smooth's `design` into the combined $X$ and writes each smooth's
penalty into its own diagonal block of a stacked penalty tensor, recording the
column `slices` so each block knows which coefficients it owns. The optional
`parametric=` argument and the intercept enter as unpenalised columns — the zero
block above.

A random effect is not a special case in this code; it is *one more smooth*.
`re_smooth(g, by=...)` (in `basis.py`) returns an `REBasis` whose `design` is the
one-hot indicator `one_hot(g)` of the grouping factor (or `one_hot(g) * by` for a
random *slope*) and whose `penalty` is the **identity** — a ridge. Its docstring
states the equivalence outright: the single Fellner–Schall smoothing parameter on
this block "**is** the random-effect precision $\lambda = 1/\sigma_b^2$", and its
fitted block coefficients are the BLUPs (best linear unbiased predictors). Because
`REBasis` is a third `Smooth` variant alongside `SplineBasis` and
`TensorBasis`, it "slots straight into `gam_fit` with **no new solver work**." Our
age-by-site model is therefore literally
`gam_fit(Y, smooths=[bspline_basis(age), re_smooth(site)], ...)`.

Two structured variants are the same idea with shaped designs. `by_factor_smooth(x, by=f)`
builds a *separate* penalised smooth of `x` per level of a factor `f` — returning a
tuple of `SplineBasis` blocks, each level's design carrying nonzero rows only for
its own observations, so the blocks stay orthogonal in $X^{\top}WX$ and each gets
its own $\lambda$. `varying_coefficient_smooth(x, by=z)` for a *continuous* `z`
builds the term $z\cdot f(x)$ — a coefficient on `z` that varies smoothly with `x`
— by scaling the P-spline design of `x` row-wise by `z`, leaving the roughness
penalty unchanged. In every case the output is another `(design, penalty)` block
the same machinery consumes.

## One loop selects them all {#sec-oneloop}

We now have $K$ tuning parameters — one $\lambda_k$ per smooth, one $1/\sigma_b^2$
per random effect — and they must be chosen *together*, because they trade off
against each other (smooth the age curve too hard and its residual inflates the
site variance). The [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd) page derived the update
that does this; here it runs once per block.

**Why it decouples cleanly.** The general Fellner–Schall step for block $k$ is

$$
\lambda_k \leftarrow \lambda_k\,
  \frac{\operatorname{tr}\!\big(S(\lambda)^{-}S_k\big) - \operatorname{tr}(V S_k)}
       {\gamma_k^{\top}S_k\gamma_k\,/\,\phi},
$$ {#eq-fs}

where $V=(X^{\top}WX+S(\lambda))^{-1}$ is the influence matrix, $\phi$ the
dispersion, and $S(\lambda)^{-}$ a generalised inverse. The numerator compares how
much *room* the penalty leaves the block ($\operatorname{tr}(S(\lambda)^{-}S_k)$,
loosely the model degrees of freedom the block could use) against how much the
*fit* actually uses ($\operatorname{tr}(VS_k)$); the denominator is the realised
penalty energy of the block. The ratio nudges $\lambda_k$ up when the block is
using less wiggle than it is allowed and down when it is straining against the
penalty — exactly the REML logic for a variance component, now reading "smoothing
parameter" or "random-effect precision" indifferently.

The expensive-looking piece is $\operatorname{tr}(S(\lambda)^{-}S_k)$, which in
general needs the generalised inverse of the *summed* penalty. But because the
blocks are disjoint (the [disjoint-block lemma](fellner-schall.qmd#the-update)),
this trace collapses to $\operatorname{rank}(S_k)/\lambda_k$ — read straight off
the block's own rank, no pseudo-inverse of $S(\lambda)$ required. `nitrix` keeps
each block's penalty eigenvalues precomputed (`pen_eig`) and forms the trace as the
elementwise sum `_trace_slinv_sk`, which reduces to exactly $\operatorname{rank}_k/\lambda_k$
for a lone disjoint block and stays correct for the overlapping margins of a
tensor-product smooth. So one outer loop visits every block, applies @eq-fs to each
from its own quantities, and the inner loop is the GLM's penalised IRLS unchanged.
Every smoothing parameter and every variance component in our age-by-site model is
selected by *the same* loop, in one pass over the blocks.

The [GLMM](glmm.qmd) few-level path is the proof in code: `glmm_fit` with a small
number of groups dispatches to "the dense GAMM path — `gam_fit` with a
`re_smooth` block and the GLM family." A GLMM with few levels *is* a GAMM; the two
chapters were describing the same fit from two directions.

## The ladder, folded

Stand back and the whole suite is one statement:

> **Fit $(X^{\top}WX + S(\lambda))\,\gamma = X^{\top}Wz$ by penalised IRLS, and
> choose the $\lambda_k$ by Fellner–Schall.**

Every rung is a choice of what goes in $S(\lambda)$ — and each block is one
**inductive bias** declared as a prior: a roughness block says *smooth*, an
identity ridge says *groups cluster*, a kernel block says *nearby inputs behave
alike*. Fellner–Schall then calibrates each bias's strength from the data
(empirical Bayes). Each rung contributed exactly one part of the machinery that
makes the GAMM work:

| rung | what goes in $S(\lambda)$ | what it contributed |
|---|---|---|
| [GLM](glm.qmd) | nothing (empty penalty) | the IRLS inner loop: $W$, $z$, the link |
| [LME](lme.qmd) | identity blocks (ridges) | variance components; REML selection |
| [GAM](gam.qmd) | roughness blocks | penalised splines; the penalty $S$ |
| [GLMM](glmm.qmd) | identity blocks, non-Gaussian | integrating out the random effect under a link |
| **GAMM** (here) | roughness *and* identity blocks | the assembly: both kinds at once |
| [Gaussian process](hsgp.qmd) | a **kernel** block $S=K^{-1}$ | next rung |

The GAMM does not add a sixth algorithm; it shows the first five were one algorithm
with different blocks. That is why the suite is *one* suite, not six fitters: a
single solve, a single selector, and an interchangeable inventory of penalty
blocks.

And the last column of that table is the doorway to the [next chapter](hsgp.qmd). A
Gaussian process is nothing more than one more penalty block whose matrix is the
inverse of a covariance kernel, $S=K^{-1}$. Drop it into $S(\lambda)$ alongside the
splines and ridges and the same loop estimates its hyperparameters too. The smooth
was a random effect; the random effect was a penalty; the penalty is a kernel —
and the kernel is a Gaussian process. The loop never changes.

## Further reading

- Ruppert, D., Wand, M. P. & Carroll, R. J. (2003). *Semiparametric Regression*.
  Cambridge University Press. The foundational treatment of penalised splines as
  mixed models — the null-space/range-space decomposition of
  [the unification section](#sec-unification) is theirs, and it is the cleanest
  route into this chapter.
- Wood, S. N. (2017). *Generalized Additive Models: An Introduction with R* (2nd
  ed.). Chapman & Hall/CRC. The GAMM chapters; the practical reference for
  assembling smooths and random effects in one penalised model.
- Wood, S. N. (2004). Stable and efficient multiple smoothing parameter estimation
  for generalized additive models. *Journal of the American Statistical
  Association*, 99(467), 673–686. How to select many smoothing/variance parameters
  jointly and stably — the problem the [single selection loop](#sec-oneloop) solves.
- Wood, S. N. (2011). Fast stable restricted maximum likelihood and marginal
  likelihood estimation of semiparametric generalized linear models. *Journal of
  the Royal Statistical Society: Series B*, 73(1), 3–36. The REML view that ties
  the smoothing-parameter and variance-component estimation together.

## See also

- Code: [`nitrix/stats/gam.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/gam.py)
  (`gam_fit`, the `_assemble` block-diagonal penalty builder, the Fellner–Schall
  trace `_trace_slinv_sk`),
  [`nitrix/stats/basis.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/basis.py)
  (`re_smooth`/`REBasis`, `by_factor_smooth`, `varying_coefficient_smooth`),
  [`nitrix/stats/glmm/__init__.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/glmm/__init__.py)
  (the few-level GAMM path).
- The unification it rests on: [the GAM hinge](gam.qmd#sec-hinge),
  [the GAM penalty](gam.qmd#sec-penalty), the
  [grand equivalence](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence), and the
  [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd) page.
- Previous rung: [GLMM](glmm.qmd). Next rung: [Gaussian processes](hsgp.qmd).
